explanation: `mystery(7)` evaluates as `7 + 5 + 3 + mystery(1)`, and `mystery(1)` returns `2`, for a total of `17`. The other options result from treating the base case as `0` or `1`, or from including an extra recursive step.

The first print happens before each recursive call, producing `321`; the second print happens as the calls finish, producing `123`. Options B, C, and D reflect missing the post-recursive prints or using the wrong call order.

Each call moves the last character to the front and the first character to the end while recursing on the middle, so `trail` becomes `l` + `iar` + `t`, or `liart`. The distractors come from reversing all characters incorrectly or duplicating an endpoint.
`countDown(5)` calls `countDown(2)`, then `countDown(-1)`, moving farther from the base case `n == 0`. The other calls eventually reach `0` exactly.

The method compares each element with the greatest value returned from the rest of the array, so it returns the maximum value, `9`. The other options are values from the array but are not the largest.

The method recursively removes the last digit using integer division and adds that digit using `% 10`, so it computes `5 + 0 + 7 + 4 = 16`. Option A omits the leading digit, while C and D treat the method as preserving or reversing the number.
`sumSkip(7)` evaluates to `7 + 5 + 3 + 1 + 0`, which is `16`. The other choices reflect stopping too early or adding values that are not reached by the recursive calls.
Each positive call prints before and after its recursive call, so the values print while going down and again while returning. Choice D omits the second print from `show(1)`.

The method recursively processes all but the first character, then appends the first character, reversing the string. Choices C and D reflect removing or rotating characters instead of tracing the returned values.
`calc(5)` is `calc(4) + calc(3)`, which evaluates to `3 + 2 = 5`. Choice D is the next value in this pattern, and choice A is `calc(4)`.

The first middle index is `3`, and since `14 > 9`, the next search is from indexes `4` through `7`; the new middle index is `5`, where the target is found. Choice C gives the target value instead of its index.
`reduce(0)` calls `reduce(0 / 2)`, which is again `reduce(0)`, so it never reaches `n == 1`. The other calls either start at `1` or eventually divide down to `1`.
`mystery(6)` evaluates to `6 + mystery(4)`, then `6 + 4 + mystery(2)`, then `6 + 4 + 2 + mystery(0)`, and the base case returns `2`. The total is `14`.
Each recursive call removes the first letter and adds it after the result of the smaller call. This reverses the string, so `desk` becomes `ksed`.
The method prints `5`, `3`, and `1` before the recursive calls finish, then prints `1`, `3`, and `5` as the calls return. The call with `-1` prints nothing.
The recursive calls strip off digits using integer division by `10`, and each call adds the last digit using `% 10`. The sum is `4 + 7 + 0 + 5 = 16`.
Starting with `3`, the recursive argument increases to `4`, `5`, and so on, so the base case `n == 0` is never reached. The calls continue until the call stack is exhausted.
The calls are `f(30, 12)`, `f(12, 6)`, and `f(6, 0)`. The base case then returns `6`.

The method prints before and after each recursive call until `n <= 1`, so the calls print `5 3 1` going down and `3 5` while returning. The other choices omit the return-side prints or use the wrong recursive step.

`calc(4)` adds 3 four times and then reaches the base case value 2, giving `3 + 3 + 3 + 3 + 2 = 14`. The distractors reflect counting too few or too many recursive additions or ignoring the base value.

Each call removes the first one-character substring and appends it after the result of the recursive call, reversing the string. The other choices preserve the original order or move only part of the string.
The method recursively strips off the last digit and adds `n % 10`, so it computes `4 + 7 + 2 + 5 = 18`. The other choices confuse the quotient with the accumulated digit sum.
The arguments are `10`, `7`, `4`, `1`, and `-2`, for five total calls. The off-by-one distractors stop before counting the base-case call or count only some decreases.

options.label: C
options.text: It eventually reaches the base case and returns `1`.

options.label: D
options.text: It does not terminate normally because the recursive calls move away from the base case.

explanation: Starting at `3`, each recursive call uses a larger value, so `n == 1` is never reached. The numeric answers assume the argument decreases or that only one call is made.

The method prints `5`, `3`, and `1` before reaching the base case, which prints `X`; then each paused call resumes and prints `0`, `2`, and `4`. The other choices place the after-recursion prints in the wrong order or omit them.
